label: SES-054 deposit_event entity schema designed (governance-entity schema-design workstream concluded): 1 session, 6 decisions (DEC-155..160), 0 planning items, 7 references

session.title: deposit_event entity schema designed; governance-entity schema-design workstream concluded; build-planning kickoff drafted
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai sixth and final schema-design conversation in the governance-entity schema-design workstream. Produced governance-schema-specs/deposit_event.md (the deposit_event entity schema specification — the second entity in DEC-118's two-entity deposit-bucket family, completing the six-entity coverage extension from DEC-121) and governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md (the kickoff prompt for the workstream's seventh and integrating conversation). Six decisions authored at close; zero planning items (PI-022 continues to cover retroactive backfill). Established two new cross-spec precedents: born-terminal append-only with creation as the event-recording moment, and born-terminal append-only entities admit multi-event-per-target-record. Surfaced one cross-spec consistency finding for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation: close_out_payload spec's at-most-one inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge default relaxes to zero-or-more. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `deposit_event` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/deposit_event.md` — the complete schema specification per the template in `governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md`. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern. This conversation also produces one additional artifact: the kickoff prompt for the build-planning conversation that closes the workstream."

Structured summary of the architectural discussion:

The conversation drove six architectural decisions, one at a time, with full discussion at each step before the user's terse approval. The six decisions and their resolutions:

(1) DEC-155 — Identifier prefix and format. Adopted DEP-NNN zero-padded to three digits. Three-letter form on the strength of the dominant three-letter precedent (DEC, REF, COP). Pairs naturally with COP for the family-3 deposit-bucket reading. Alternatives DEPT, DEPOS, EVT, APPLY rejected for disambiguation, length, or semantic mismatch.

(2) DEC-156 — Lifecycle shape. The primary architectural decision of the conversation. The user pushed back on the initial recommendation (born-terminal append-only) by asking about continuous-status visibility for failure diagnosis. Discussion surfaced three real failure scenarios (clean failure handled equally by both options; unclean crash where workflow-staged would capture stuck-running state; long apply where continuous status would help). A third synthesis option emerged: born-terminal append-only with diagnostic log file capture via a deposit_event_log_file_path field. The user adopted the synthesis. Establishes a new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only with creation as the event-recording moment — a third lifecycle category alongside workflow-shaped and documentary.

(3) DEC-157 — Back-reference mechanism. Adopted references-table edges with one new generic-verb kind deposit_event_wrote_record spanning four target types (session, decision, planning_item, reference) in one vocabulary entry. Rejected per-target-type variants (A′), JSON column (B), dedicated back-reference table (C), target-side columns (D) on grounds of references-edge precedent, established generic-verb naming pattern, and minimum-viable schema growth. Settled cardinality, 409-SKIP semantics (skipped records not back-referenced), back-reference scope (substantive content layer only, no recursive back-references).

(4) DEC-158 — Re-apply semantics. Adopted multi-event-per-payload: a close_out_payload may carry zero or more inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edges, one per apply attempt. Establishes a second new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only entities admit multi-event-per-target-record. Relaxes the close_out_payload spec's at-most-one default (which that spec section 3.8.3 explicitly deferred to this conversation). Surfaced as a cross-spec consistency finding for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation. The close_out_payload's ready → applied transition fires on the first inbound DEP with outcome=success; subsequent DEPs (failures, re-confirmations) don't move the close_out_payload's status.

(5) DEC-159 — Field inventory. Captured the nine-column shape (identifier, title, description, outcome, records_summary, error_info, apply_context, log_file_path, created_at). Adopted _outcome over _status for semantic precision (born-terminal fact vs. workflow state). Declined _notes (incompatible with immutable record), _updated_at and _deleted_at (born-terminal append-only deviation), denormalized total-records-written count (derivable). JSON shape for records_summary, error_info, apply_context documented in spec but not SQL-enforced.

(6) DEC-160 — API surface, UI, soft-delete posture, acceptance criteria. Reduced API surface (POST + GET only). Read-only UI audit log with no Create/Edit/Delete dialogs, Outcome filter combo in master-pane toolbar, master pane sorts identifier descending (audit-log deviation), right-click context menu reduced to two read affordances. Plain-text log_file_path display (no in-app viewer). Strict no-Create-dialog (backfill via PI-022 implementation script). Fifteen acceptance criteria captured.

After all six decisions settled, the spec was drafted end-to-end at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/deposit_event.md (approximately 290 lines following the spec guide template). The build-planning conversation's kickoff prompt was drafted at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md (the additional deliverable required at this conversation's close per the kickoff prompt's framing).

The conversation surfaced no new planning items. PI-022 (authored by SES-046) continues to cover retroactive backfill of governance entity records, refined by the build-planning conversation per the deposit_event spec section 3.8.2 working assumptions. One cross-spec consistency finding surfaced for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation: the close_out_payload spec's at-most-one inbound edge default needs revision to zero-or-more.
session.artifacts_produced: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/deposit_event.md (initial draft v1.0); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md (initial draft v1.0); six decisions DEC-155 through DEC-160; seven references (six decided_in plus one is_about to PI-022); zero new planning items.
session.in_flight_at_end: Build-planning conversation queued. Kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md. The build-planning conversation consumes all six governance schema specifications (workstream, conversation, reference_book, work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) and produces the integrating Product Requirements Document (governance-entity-PRD-v0.1.md), implementation plan (governance-entity-implementation-plan.md), per-slice Claude Code build prompts, and PI-022 refinement. Target user-interface version open at this writing — to be coordinated with the active version sequence at that time. The build-planning conversation's first task is the cross-spec consistency check per spec guide section 7.2, which includes reconciling the close_out_payload at-most-one relaxation surfaced by this conversation's Decision 4. This conversation closes the schema-design portion of the governance entity schema-design workstream; the build-planning conversation closes the workstream.

decisions.title: deposit_event identifier prefix and format: DEP-NNN
decisions.context: The deposit_event entity is the sixth and final governance entity type designed in the governance-entity schema-design workstream. Per the spec guide section 6 cross-spec consistency conventions, the identifier prefix is 2-to-5 uppercase letters, zero-padded to three digits, and must not collide with the existing prefix list (DEC, SES, RSK, PI, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC, ENG, WS, CONV, RB, WT, COP). The kickoff prompt named DEP as the working assumption with DEPOS, DEPT, EVT, APPLY as alternatives raised.
decisions.decision: Adopt DEP as the identifier prefix with format DEP-NNN zero-padded to three digits (e.g., DEP-001, DEP-054).
decisions.rationale: Three-letter form matches the dominant three-letter precedent (DEC, REF, COP, plus the two-letter exceptions WS, WT, RB and the four-letter PROC). Pairs naturally with COP for the family-3 deposit-bucket reading: a COP-NNN payload is applied by DEP-NNN-or-other events. Survives the collision check against the current prefix list. Per DEC-123's affirmation that each downstream conversation makes its own prefix-length call within the 2-to-5 letter range, three letters is appropriate here.
decisions.alternatives_considered: DEPT (four letters) — rejected for visual collision with 'Department' in unrelated contexts. DEPOS (five letters) — rejected for length-without-disambiguation; no parallel five-letter precedent exists in governance or methodology. EVT (three letters) — rejected for reading as generic 'event'; loses the deposit-bucket family connotation. APPLY (five letters) — rejected for reading as a verb rather than entity name; collides with the apply script and apply-close-out prompt series naming. DP (two letters) — not seriously considered (too short; reads as initials).
decisions.consequences: DEP becomes the canonical identifier prefix for the new entity type. Format DEP-NNN matches every other governance prefixed identifier in V2. No vocabulary or naming conflicts surface in the cross-spec consistency check. The COP/DEP pairing makes the family-3 deposit-bucket reading visually obvious in audit queries and database snapshots.

decisions.title: deposit_event lifecycle shape: born-terminal append-only with diagnostic log capture (new cross-spec precedent)
decisions.context: The primary architectural decision of the conversation. The kickoff prompt named a four-state workflow lifecycle (running, success, failure, aborted) as a working assumption while explicitly noting that 'the deposit_event entity is expected to introduce the append-only-with-one-timestamp precedent' per the close_out_payload spec section 3.4.6 and work_ticket spec section 3.4.3 anticipations. The choice cascades into status/outcome field semantics, timestamp count, API surface (PUT/PATCH admitted or not), and UI shape (read-only audit log versus editable workflow panel). The user pushed back on the initial recommendation by asking about continuous-status visibility for failure diagnosis, prompting a synthesis option to be put on the table.
decisions.decision: Adopt born-terminal append-only with diagnostic log capture. The deposit_event record is POSTed by the apply script at apply completion with outcome and all diagnostic fields populated; no intermediate 'running' state; no PUT, no PATCH, no DELETE; single timestamp (deposit_event_created_at) carrying the event moment. A new deposit_event_log_file_path field (repo-relative path per the file-pointer precedent from reference_book/work_ticket/close_out_payload) points at a stdout transcript captured during apply, providing durable diagnostic detail without status-transition machinery.
decisions.rationale: Born-terminal append-only realizes the precedent anticipated by predecessor specs (close_out_payload section 3.4.6, work_ticket section 3.4.3), matches the operational shape of apply (creation IS the event), and yields the smallest possible API and UI surface for an entity whose purpose is durable auditing rather than workflow tracking. The 'running' intermediate state has no operational meaning for a 3-second apply and would introduce edit machinery incompatible with the fact-recording intent. The log file synthesis addresses the user's continuous-status concern by capturing diagnostic detail in a separate durable artifact (the log file) rather than forcing the entity itself into a workflow lifecycle. The synthesis preserves the structural simplicity of born-terminal append-only while addressing the legitimate diagnostic visibility need that motivated the user's pushback. Establishes a new cross-spec precedent — born-terminal append-only with creation as the event-recording moment — a third lifecycle category alongside workflow-shaped (workstream, conversation, close_out_payload, work_ticket) and documentary (reference_book).
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B — workflow-staged multi-status (running → success/failure/aborted, with per-status timestamps, PUT/PATCH admitted during running, edit dialogs for the status transition). Rejected on the grounds that (a) the 'running' state lasts ~3 seconds and is essentially never observed in operation, (b) PUT/PATCH endpoints and editable dialogs contradict the fact-about-an-event intuition the entity is meant to capture, (c) the unclean-crash visibility benefit it would provide is small relative to the cost (apply runs interactively in ~3 seconds; crashes are rare and the terminal output is right there), (d) it would reopen the precedent the predecessor specs anticipated. Option A — born-terminal append-only without log capture. Rejected after the user's pushback as too thin on diagnostic detail: a crashed-mid-flight apply would leave no governance record and no log file to consult; the operator would have only the (potentially-closed) terminal output. The Option C synthesis (born-terminal append-only WITH log capture) addresses both concerns.
decisions.consequences: Establishes a new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only with creation as the event-recording moment. A third lifecycle category for governance entities. Cascading effects: deposit_event has _outcome (not _status), single _created_at timestamp (no per-status timestamps), POST + GET only API surface, no PUT/PATCH/DELETE, no soft-delete, read-only UI with no Create/Edit/Delete dialogs. The apply script (apply_close_out.py) requires modification per Slice D of the build-planning conversation: write stdout to a log file during apply, POST the deposit_event record at end with outcome and all diagnostic fields populated. A new directory (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/) is established as the canonical log file home; whether files there are git-tracked or gitignored is a build-planning policy decision.

decisions.title: deposit_event back-reference mechanism: references-table edges with one new generic-verb kind deposit_event_wrote_record
decisions.context: Each apply attempt creates zero or more records in the database (a session, decisions, planning_items, references, possibly other governance entity types in the future). The deposit_event record needs to point at those records so 'what did this apply create?' and 'which apply created this decision?' are first-class governance queries. The kickoff prompt named four alternative mechanisms: references-table edges (the working assumption per the established DEC-124 references-edge cross-spec precedent), JSON column on the deposit_events table, dedicated back-reference table, or target-side columns. The references-table option further admits a sub-choice between a single generic kind and per-target-type kinds.
decisions.decision: Adopt references-table edges with one new generic-verb cross-cutting kind, deposit_event_wrote_record, spanning four target types (session, decision, planning_item, reference) in a single vocabulary entry and a single _kinds_for_pair clause covering all four pairs.
decisions.rationale: Continues the DEC-124 references-edge-over-foreign-key cross-spec precedent locked by SES-048. Adds the minimum viable schema growth (one vocab entry, one pair rule). Supports both directional queries via the existing references-table machinery: 'which records did DEP-001 write?' (forward) and 'which DEP wrote DEC-117?' (reverse) are both single refs-table queries. Single generic kind chosen over per-target-type variants on the strength of (a) cross-cutting nature — the relationship intentionally spans multiple target types and a single conceptual kind captures that intent; (b) established generic-verb naming pattern in the existing vocabulary (references, is_about, supersedes, decided_in all use generic-verb naming without target-type binding); (c) minimum-viable schema growth. Per DEC-133's frequency-justified test, the generic single-kind variant is the right choice for this cross-cutting cross-target-type relationship.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Per-target-type kinds within references-table (deposit_event_wrote_session, deposit_event_wrote_decision, etc., four kinds, four pair clauses) — rejected for unnecessary vocabulary growth when a single conceptual relationship is being modeled. JSON column on deposit_events table (deposit_event_records_written array of target_type/target_id pairs) — rejected for bypassing V2's relationship-vocabulary discipline, making the reverse query JSON-search-bound rather than a direct refs-table query. Dedicated back-reference table (new deposit_event_records table with deposit_event_identifier, target_type, target_id columns) — rejected for introducing a parallel relationship-storage mechanism outside the references-table discipline without compensating benefit. Target-side back-reference columns (written_by_deposit_event_identifier on each of the five target tables) — rejected for inverting the references-edge precedent, requiring migrations on established methodology/governance entity tables, and making the forward query a fan-out across all target tables.
decisions.consequences: One new vocabulary entry in REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS: deposit_event_wrote_record. One new _kinds_for_pair clause: source_type='deposit_event' and target_type in ('session', 'decision', 'planning_item', 'reference') admits the kind. Plus the parent-linkage kind deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload from DEC-159's field inventory. Apply script flow at end-of-apply: step 5 creates the deposit_event record, step 6 emits one back-reference edge per HTTP-200/201-created record (409-SKIPped records are not back-referenced). Cardinality: one DEP → many records (zero or more); one record → zero or one DEP (zero if created outside the apply path, one if created by apply). The back-reference edges themselves are NOT recursively back-referenced (the recursion stops at the substantive content layer). Future expansion to additional target entity types is one-line additions to the _kinds_for_pair clause.

decisions.title: deposit_event re-apply semantics: multi-event-per-payload (new cross-spec precedent)
decisions.context: The close_out_payload spec section 3.8.3 adopted at-most-one inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge as the safe default and explicitly deferred the call to this conversation. DEC-156's born-terminal append-only shape forces the question into the open: each apply attempt either creates a new immutable DEP record (multi-event), or the apply script refuses to create a second one (at-most-one). The apply script is already idempotent on re-run (HTTP 409 SKIPs for already-present records), and re-apply is an operationally meaningful pattern (verification, retry-after-partial-failure, debugging). The choice affects edge cardinality, the close_out_payload's status transition rule, and what audit history the deposit_events table captures.
decisions.decision: Adopt multi-event-per-payload. Each apply attempt creates a new deposit_event record. A close_out_payload may carry zero or more inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edges, one per apply attempt. The close_out_payload's ready → applied transition fires on the first inbound DEP with outcome=success; subsequent DEPs (failures or re-confirmations) do not move the close_out_payload's status (it's already at applied and terminal per the truly-terminal precedent). Failed DEPs against a ready close_out_payload create a governance record of the failed attempt but do not transition the close_out_payload.
decisions.rationale: Naturally pairs with born-terminal append-only (DEC-156): each attempt is a fact about an event; multiple attempts are multiple facts. Aligns with the operational reality of re-apply (verification, retry, redeploy). Captures the full apply history of a payload as a governance-queryable artifact. The at-most-one alternative would force re-apply attempts into ghost runs with no governance record, contradicting the audit-log purpose of the deposit_events table. Establishes a new cross-spec precedent — born-terminal append-only entities admit multi-event-per-target-record — useful for any future workstream that revisits this producer/consumer pair shape.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B — at-most-one (one DEP per COP, single-success rule). The apply script checks for an existing successful DEP and refuses to create a second one. Rejected because it forces re-apply attempts into ghost runs with no governance record, contradicting the audit purpose of the entity and the close_out_payload spec section 3.8.3's invitation to relax. Option C — at-most-one-success-but-multiple-failures. First successful apply creates DEP-1; subsequent successful re-applies refused; failed applies admitted. Rejected because the distinction between 'successful re-apply' and 'failed re-apply' has no operational meaning at the access layer (a successful re-confirmation IS valuable governance information; refusing to record it is artificial).
decisions.consequences: Establishes a new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only entities admit multi-event-per-target-record. Surfaces a cross-spec consistency finding for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation: the close_out_payload spec section 3.3.2 and section 3.4.3 carry at-most-one language that needs revision to zero-or-more. Per the kickoff's no-inline-amendment rule, this conversation does not edit close_out_payload.md; the revision is handled at the build-planning conversation's consistency gate. The deposit_events table grows linearly with apply attempts (not exponentially); most COPs will have exactly one DEP, some debugged ones will have a small number. The access layer's atomic transaction on POST /deposit-events with outcome=success against a ready COP transitions the COP atomically; POSTs against an already-applied COP succeed without COP transition; POSTs with outcome=failure leave the COP unchanged regardless of pre-state.

decisions.title: deposit_event field inventory: nine columns including three diagnostic JSON fields; _outcome over _status; declines _notes, _updated_at, _deleted_at, denormalized total count
decisions.context: Field inventory is mostly cascading from prior decisions (DEC-156 born-terminal append-only fixes timestamp shape; DEC-157 fixes relationship modeling — no foreign-key columns). The conversation surfaced four sub-choices warranting explicit settlement: outcome field name (semantic precision vs. vocabulary consistency); inclusion of title/description fields for parity with predecessor entities; JSON shape lock-in for the three diagnostic JSON fields; base-schema timestamp deviation under full append-only.
decisions.decision: Adopt the nine-column shape: deposit_event_identifier, deposit_event_title (auto-generated by apply script), deposit_event_description (auto-generated), deposit_event_outcome (enum success|failure), deposit_event_records_summary (JSON), deposit_event_error_info (JSON, conditional null/non-null on outcome), deposit_event_apply_context (JSON), deposit_event_log_file_path (repo-relative), deposit_event_created_at. Adopt _outcome over _status for semantic precision. Document the three JSON shapes (records_summary, error_info, apply_context) in spec language but not at the SQL constraint layer; Python helper module shares the shape between apply script and access layer. Deviate from base-schema timestamps: no _updated_at (record is immutable), no _deleted_at (no soft-delete; full append-only). Decline _notes (incompatible with immutable record), denormalized total-records-written count (derivable from records_summary sum or wrote_record edge count).
decisions.rationale: Identity and content fields (identifier, title, description) provide parity with predecessor entities and avoid runtime computation in master-pane list rendering; auto-generation by the apply script removes consultant authoring overhead. _outcome field name reflects the semantic shape — born-terminal fact about an event rather than workflow state implying transitions; the naming distinction is the precedent being established. Three JSON diagnostic fields capture the rich detail of an apply attempt without committing to a fixed column schema that would need migration for shape evolution; SQL-level shape enforcement adds engine-specific complexity without compensating benefit (Python validator pattern is the canonical enforcement elsewhere in V2). Base-schema deviation on _updated_at and _deleted_at is the precedent being established — born-terminal append-only entities omit update and delete semantics. Field declines (_notes, denormalized count) follow from the entity nature (immutable record, derivable summary).
decisions.alternatives_considered: _status field name (vocabulary consistency with workstream/conversation/close_out_payload/work_ticket) — rejected because the field's semantic shape is permanent fact, not workflow state; naming should follow semantics. Include _notes field — rejected because consultant scratchpad pattern is incompatible with immutable record. SQL-level JSON shape enforcement via SQLite's JSON1 extension — rejected for portability and engine-specific complexity. Retain base-schema _updated_at and _deleted_at columns locked to null/created — admissible at implementation but the spec declares no semantics (build-planning chooses implementation path). Denormalize total-records-written as a separate column — rejected as premature optimization; derivable from records_summary or edge count.
decisions.consequences: The deposit_events table has nine columns total. The records_summary JSON shape is canonically {sessions: int, decisions: int, planning_items: int, references: int} with additive extension admitted. The error_info JSON shape carries kind, message, step, optional http_status, optional traceback when outcome=failure; null when outcome=success (access-layer cross-check). The apply_context JSON shape carries apply_script_version, invocation, runner. The log_file_path stores repo-relative paths under the canonical pattern PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/dep_NNN.log. Field-naming convention break (_outcome rather than _status) is documented in the spec as a deliberate deviation; build-planning's section 7.2 consistency check confirms acceptance as the new precedent rather than violation.

decisions.title: deposit_event API surface, UI shape, soft-delete posture, fifteen acceptance criteria: minimum-viable read-only audit log
decisions.context: API surface and UI shape cascade from prior decisions (DEC-156 born-terminal append-only forces POST + GET only; the read-only nature forces no Create/Edit/Delete dialogs). The conversation surfaced three sub-choices warranting explicit settlement: master-pane sort order (ascending consistency vs. descending audit-log nature); log-file viewer affordance (plain-text path vs. in-app button); backfill authoring path (strict no-Create-dialog vs. admit dialog for PI-022 backfill).
decisions.decision: Adopt reduced API surface (POST + GET only, no PUT, no PATCH, no DELETE, no /restore). Adopt read-only UI audit log with no Create/Edit/Delete/Restore dialogs. Master pane: ListDetailPanel with columns identifier, title, outcome (badge-styled), created_at; Outcome filter combo (All/Success/Failure) in the master-pane toolbar; default sort identifier descending (audit-log deviation from V2's default ascending). Detail pane: vertical read-only layout rendering all fields plus the references section with parent-linkage edge prominent at top and wrote_record edges grouped by target_type with count badges; 'Add reference' affordance disabled. Right-click context menu reduced to two lightweight read affordances: Copy identifier, Copy log path. Plain-text log_file_path display with no in-app viewer in this release. Strict no-Create-dialog posture: PI-022 backfill execution via one-off script (Slice E), not via UI. Fifteen acceptance criteria captured covering schema migration, vocabulary additions, POST validation, conditional error_info rule, parent-linkage and back-reference edge handling, atomic close_out_payload transition on success, multi-event-per-payload semantics, records-summary cross-check, method-not-allowed responses, GET envelope and filter, UI panel placement and behavior, apply script modification.
decisions.rationale: Born-terminal append-only (DEC-156) forces POST + GET only — no update or delete operations have meaning. Read-only UI follows from the same precedent — the apply script is the canonical authoring path; there is no consultant authoring flow for new records. Master-pane sort descending matches the audit-log read pattern (newest first) and is itself a recognizable signal that this is a log, not a workspace; documented deviation tied to entity nature. Outcome filter combo parallels work_ticket and close_out_payload's Status filter combo on the operational browse-by-classification pattern. Plain-text log_file_path display defers in-app viewer machinery as a future-add pending real friction signal; copying the path to a terminal is two seconds of friction. Strict no-Create-dialog keeps the 'deposit_event is a fact captured by the apply script' intuition clean; PI-022's backfill scope is a build-planning concern handled via one-off script per the predecessor pattern (apply_ses_011_planning_records.py and similar).
decisions.alternatives_considered: Master-pane sort ascending (cross-panel consistency) — rejected for working against the audit-log read pattern. 'View log' button in detail pane opening the file in the platform's default viewer — rejected as introducing filesystem-access machinery that doesn't earn its complexity for a 30-record-lifetime entity; future-add if real friction emerges. UI Create dialog used exclusively for backfill — rejected as muddying the 'apply script is the only authoring path' intuition; the backfill script approach follows established precedent (apply_ses_011_planning_records.py).
decisions.consequences: deposit_events route table registers POST and GET endpoints only. PUT, PATCH, DELETE, /restore return HTTP 405 (or HTTP 404 if framework auto-resolves; implementation choice for build-planning). Detail pane wires no Create/Edit/Delete/Restore dialogs and disables the references-create dialog's 'Add reference' affordance. Right-click context menu offers two read affordances. Master pane default sort is identifier descending. Outcome filter combo and badge styling for outcome are wired. Fifteen acceptance criteria establish the build-planning conversation's per-slice acceptance list aggregation baseline. PI-022 backfill is Slice E of the build-planning conversation's implementation plan, executed as a one-off script after the build slices land.
